stories.headline: BRENT CRACKS $106 AS STRAIT OF HORMUZ STAYS FUNCTIONALLY CLOSED
stories.body: Brent topped $106/bbl and WTI pushed to $96 after the U.S. Navy seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship, escalating the 'dual blockade.' The IEA is now calling the Hormuz closure the largest supply disruption in oil-market history — roughly 20% of global crude flow and significant LNG volumes are offline. Brent is up ~55% since the war began.

stories.headline: U.S.–IRAN DEADLOCK DRIVES RISK-OFF INTO WEEKEND
stories.body: Equities faded and Treasuries sold off in tandem as traders priced supply-side inflation alongside growth risk. S&P 500 closed -0.37%, Nasdaq -0.51%, with energy and defensives leading while megacap tech lagged. Global bond markets continued to weaken on stagflation concerns reminiscent of the 1970s shock.

stories.headline: 10Y YIELD JUMPS TO 4.38% AS OIL SHOCK REPRICES INFLATION PATH
stories.body: The 10Y climbed ~7 bps to 4.38% and the 2Y held near 3.85%, flattening slightly but with both ends higher. Breakevens widened sharply — the market is now doing the Fed's tightening for it via the term premium, even as real activity indicators soften.

stories.headline: FED STILL PENCILED IN ONE CUT AS POWELL FACES SUPPLY-SHOCK DILEMMA
stories.body: The FOMC held at 3.50–3.75% in March and the dot plot still projected one 2026 cut, but fed funds futures now imply the first cut pushed to September or later. Powell's challenge: a classic supply shock lifts inflation and hurts growth simultaneously — the textbook 'do nothing and look through it' answer is politically harder when headline CPI is visibly spiking at the pump.

stories.headline: GOLD COOLS FROM RECORD AFTER $4,738 PEAK; DXY BOUNCES
stories.body: Spot gold slipped ~1% to $4,689 after printing an all-time high of $4,738 yesterday, as the dollar index rebounded +0.3% to 98.82 on safe-haven bid and higher real yields. The move suggests tactical profit-taking rather than a regime change — gold's year-to-date move remains the dominant macro trade.

stories.headline: NBA DRAFT DECLARATION DEADLINE TONIGHT AS PORTAL CLOSES
stories.body: Underclassmen have until 11:59 PM ET tonight to declare for the NBA Draft, following Tuesday's transfer portal deadline. Six of 247Sports' top 10 transfers remain uncommitted, headlined by Iowa State sharpshooter Milan Momcilovic. Notable landing spots: JT Toppin to Houston, Jackson Shelstad to Louisville, Haggerty (23.4 PPG, 4th in NCAA) to Texas A&M.

stories.headline: HAWKS TAKE 2-1 SERIES LEAD; RAPTORS AVOID SWEEP WITH 126-104 BLOWOUT
stories.body: Atlanta beat the Knicks in Game 3 behind Jalen Johnson's 24-10-8 and 21 from Jonathan Kuminga, with CJ McCollum's late clutch bucket sealing it. Toronto thumped Cleveland 126-104 on 33-point games from Scottie Barnes (11 ast) and RJ Barrett (6 threes). Timberwolves also took a 2-1 lead on the Nuggets.

stories.headline: DRAFT DAY 2 OPENS AFTER MENDOZA GOES #1, SEAHAWKS GRAB JADARIAN PRICE AT 32
stories.body: Rounds 2-3 begin tonight. Round 1 saw Fernando Mendoza (Indiana QB) go #1 to the Raiders, David Bailey (Texas Tech EDGE) #2 to the Jets, and Jeremiyah Love (Notre Dame RB) #3 to Arizona. Seattle closed Round 1 by taking Love's Irish teammate Jadarian Price at #32 to replace Kenneth Walker III, who left for KC in free agency.

stories.headline: SPRING WRAPS UNDER NEW SINGLE-WINDOW PORTAL REGIME
stories.body: With the NCAA's elimination of the spring transfer portal this year, April is the first true off-season in a decade without roster churn — forcing programs to lock in their depth during the January window. Compliance eyes are on schools suspected of circumventing rules via post-spring-game 'withdrawals,' with the NCAA set to vote on harsher penalties soon.

sports.detail: Beat Magic in Game 2 to even series 1-1. Cunningham 27/11 ast, team blocked 11 shots. Game 3 Saturday in Orlando.
Rough start to the season, currently below .500. Series vs. Athletics ongoing. Offense inconsistent; rotation holding up.
Selected Jadarian Price (Notre Dame RB) at #32 to replace Kenneth Walker III. Round 2 pick at #64 tonight; also hold picks at #96 and #188.
Spring practice complete. RB Jayden Limar out recovering from ankle surgery. HC Jedd Fisch taking over as OC after parting with Jimmie Dougherty.
Sprinkle signed G Parker Friedrichsen (Davidson, 3 yrs D1) and G Steele Venters (Gonzaga, 36.7% 3PT). Roster rebuild ongoing after Diallo, Mandaquit, Steinbach, Tucker departures.

watchlist: Core PCE release Thursday 4/30 — market looking for any signal of oil pass-through into core services
May 5-6 FOMC meeting — no cut expected; watch statement language on supply shocks and Powell presser
NFL Draft Day 2 tonight 7 PM ET (Rounds 2-3) — Seahawks on the clock at #64
Pistons vs. Magic Game 3 Saturday — must-win territory for road team Detroit
Mariners vs. Athletics Saturday — Castillo expected to start, bullpen thin going into weekend

deepDive.title: Why an Oil Supply Shock Is the Hardest Call the Fed Ever Faces
content: The Strait of Hormuz closure is a textbook supply-side shock, and today's tape — equities down, yields up, gold off its high as the dollar firms — is the market trying to price a policy regime that hasn't existed in four decades. The FOMC held at 3.50–3.75% in March with one cut still in the dots, but fed funds futures are now pushing the first cut to September. The question isn't where rates go next; it's which leg of the dual mandate Powell chooses to defend when the shock forces a trade-off between them. Understanding why that trade-off is so brutal requires unpacking what a supply shock actually does to the Phillips-curve framework the Fed operates in.

A demand shock moves output and inflation in the same direction — recession cools both, boom heats both — so the Fed's reaction function is unambiguous. A supply shock moves them in opposite directions. When Brent rallies 55% because barrels are physically stuck, headline CPI rises (oil is ~3% of the core PCE basket but passes through to transport, food, and manufactured goods) while real disposable income falls, crimping demand for everything else. The Fed can raise rates to crush the secondary inflation (inflation expectations, wage demands) but that deepens the output hit. Or it can cut to cushion growth but risk un-anchoring the expectations it spent 2022–24 re-anchoring. Neither tool fixes the actual problem, which is that oil tankers aren't moving.

The historical template most invoked is 1973–74, when OPEC's embargo quadrupled crude and Arthur Burns kept policy too easy, arguably seeding the Great Inflation. The less-discussed counter-example is 1990–91: Iraq invaded Kuwait, oil doubled in three months, and Greenspan explicitly looked through it — he cut rates into the shock because the Fed judged the price move as transitory and demand as already weakening. Core inflation stayed contained, the recession was mild, and the credibility gamble paid off. The difference wasn't the size of the shock; it was the starting point. Burns inherited un-anchored expectations and a loose fiscal stance. Greenspan had residual disinflation credibility from Volcker. Powell's starting point is somewhere in between — expectations are anchored but barely, after the 2022 spike, and fiscal policy is expansionary.

That's why the 10Y jumped 7 bps today while equities sold off: bond traders are pricing the Fed to choose credibility over growth, which means 'higher for longer' even if the real economy softens. Watch three things into the May 5-6 meeting. First, the statement's treatment of 'supply-side' language — explicit acknowledgment signals the Fed is preparing to look through headline CPI. Second, the dot plot's 2027 terminal rate, which tells you whether officials think this is a one-off price level reset or a persistent regime change. Third, breakeven inflation at the 5Y5Y point — if it stays anchored near 2.3%, Powell has room to cut when growth rolls over. If it breaks above 2.6%, he's locked into hikes regardless of what GDP does. The whole policy path runs through that number.
